If any Officer, who is employed in the civil administration, has taken furlough in the Military Department under the said rules of 1875, this furlough shall be treated, in calculating the furlough or special leave admissible to him under the preceding rule, as if it had been furlough taken under the Civil Leave Code. Leave so taken under the 10th Military Rule shall be treated as if it had been privilege leave taken under the Civil Leave Code, and the claims of the Officer under the Civil Leave Code adjusted accordingly.

V. If an Officer, who has been subject to the rules in the Civil Leave Code under the third of these rules, is again employed in the Military Department, and so again becomes subject to the Military Furlough and Leave Rules of 1875, any furlough or special leave taken by him under the Civil Leave Code will be treated in calculating the furlough admissible to him under the said military rules as if it had been furlough taken under the said military rules. Privilege leave taken under the Civil Leave Code Rules will be treated as if it had been leave taken under the 10th Military Rule. Subsidiary or examination leave taken under the Civil Leave Code will not affect the claims of such an Officer under the Military Furlough and Leave Rules.

LEAVE RULES FOR THE INDIAN STAFF CORPS.

(Published in Army Circulars, India, 16 April 1886.)

1. An officer may be granted,—
Privilege leave under such regulations as may from time to time be in force.

Leave out of India, for no longer period than one year, capable of extension to two years' absence from duty, on the following pay:—

After appointment to the Staff Corps £200 a year.

After the commencement of the 5th
years' service for pension ... 250 „
Ditto, ditto, 15th ditto, ditto, ditto ... 300 „
Ditto, ditto, 20th ditto, ditto, ditto ... 450 „
Ditto, ditto, 25th ditto, ditto, ditto ... 600 „
Ditto, ditto, 30th ditto, ditto, ditto ... 700 „

Leave in India, but for the period of one year only, on full military pay and half the Staff salary of appointment.

2. No extension of leave involving absence from duty for more than two years, whether taken in or out of India, can be granted except on specially urgent grounds and without pay.

3. An officer, unable on account of the state of his health to return to duty within the maximum period of two years' absence, will, unless he is under Rule 2 specially granted an extension of leave without pay, be placed on temporary half pay or the retired list, as the circumstances of the case may seem to require. An officer will also be liable to be placed on half pay or the retired list should his health require an undue amount of leave, whether in or out of India. This power will be exercised at the instance of the Government of his own Presidency should the officer be in India, or, should he be at home, by the Secretary of State in Council.

4. All leave in or out of India will count as service for pension.

5. Leave, whether it is leave in India or leave out of India, will not involve forfeiture of appointment, and may be granted at any time, but solely at the discretion of the Commanders-in-Chief of the several armies for officers serving directly under their orders, and by the Governor-General in Council or the Governors in Council for officers of civil departments of the Army, or of Corps not under the direct orders of a Commander-in-Chief.

6. An officer on leave, whether in India or out of India, will be required to rejoin at once on being recalled to duty, unless certified by a Medical Board as unfit to do so.

7. The above rules shall apply to—
Officers whose admission to the Staff Corps shall bear date subsequent to the 30th June 1886.
Officers of the Staff Corps, including proba-

tioners, or of the Cavalry and Infantry list, of the Indian Army, in military employment, who may elect them within twelve months of that date. Officers applying for leave of any kind within that period must elect on making their application. Officers already on leave at date of promulgation must elect at once, and the new rules will apply to them from the date on which their notification of acceptance shall be received at either army headquarters or by the Government of India, or by the Local Governments, or at the India Office at home, as the case may be.

Officers in civil employ on that date, subsequently reverting to military duty, who may elect them within six months from the date of being replaced at the disposal of the Military Department, and such officers who may be on leave when so replaced must make their election at once. Those taking leave within the six months must make their election before going on leave.

They shall not apply to—
Officers in civil employ.

Officers commanding divisions, districts, or brigades, or holding other appointments under special regulations as to leave of absence, who will continue subject to such regulations.

8. Officers who, under Rule 7, elect to come under them will count all previous leave as service for pension. The position of those who do not so elect will remain unchanged.

9. The Secretary of State in Council reserves the right of making any change in the foregoing regulations which circumstances may require.

INDIAN MEDICAL SERVICE.

The above rules apply equally to officers of the Indian Medical Service in military employ, with the following modification in regard to the rates of pay whilst on leave out of India:—

After arrival in India ... .. £200 a year.

After the commencement of the 5th
year's service for pension ... 250 „
Ditto, ditto, 10th ditto ... 300 „
Ditto, ditto, 15th ditto ... 400 „
Ditto, ditto, 20th ditto ... 450 „
Ditto, ditto, 25th ditto ... 500 „

The rules will apply to all Medical Officers admitted to the Service after the 30th June 1886, and to officers who may elect them under the provisions of Rule 7.

(India Army Circulars, June 1887, Clause 90.)

With reference to Rule VII. and Clause 2 of Rule IX. of the leave rules for the Staff Corps published in Clause 51, India Army Circulars, 1886, it is notified that any officer in military employment (including medical officers),

whose election of leave rules shall not have been received by the 1st July, 1887, will be held to have finally declined those rules.

DECISIONS ON THE LEAVE RULES FOR THE STAFF CORPS.

(Published in India Army Circulars,* subsequently to the promulgation of the Rules.)

1. Officers in civil employment, while they continue in such employment, are not affected by these rules; but if desirous of retiring on pension direct from such employment, they will, whether on duty or on furlough, be given the benefit of the rule which allows all previous leave to count as service for pension.

It is, however, to be clearly understood that no officer in civil employment will be allowed to place himself out of employment for the purpose of taking leave under these rules, or for any purpose but immediate retirement.

2. There is no subsidiary leave under these rules. Leave under them commences from the date of an officer's being struck off duty, and terminates on the date he rejoins his regiment or appointment, the only exception being in case of leave out of India recommended by a Medical Board, when the period granted is held to commence from the date of the officer's quitting India, and to terminate on, and include, the date previous to that of his rejoining his regiment or appointment. But under any circumstances the absence from duty, which is restricted to two years with pay (Rule 2), will reckon from the date of an officer being struck off duty.

3. When leave "in and out of India" is granted to an officer on medical certificate in continuation of leave "in India," the former will reckon from the date of termination of the latter, subject to the limit of two years' absence from duty.

4. The term "date of being struck off duty" is intended to mean the date following the day an officer is relieved of his duties. But in the case of an officer obliged to leave his station to appear before a Medical Board at another station, the date of being "struck off duty" will mean the date he is permitted to quit the station where he is examined and invalided.

Up to date of leaving India, and from date of return to India to date of rejoining regiment or appointment, officers granted leave out of India under the new rules will receive Indian pay and half staff salary.

5. The Indian Pension Regulations are unaffected by these leave rules, except that all leave from date of commencement of service for pension will, under them, be allowed to count as service for pension.

6. Time passed at Addiscombe after the attainment of the age of 16 years will reckon as service for pension in calculating the rates of pay under Rule I.

7. The periods of service for pension in Rule I. of the leave rules will entitle an officer taking leave to the pay fixed for such periods of service, without any limitation based on the rule which prevents an officer from retiring on the pension fixed for a particular period of service, unless he has served half of that service in the Staff Corps.

8. The provisions of the rules with regard to duration of leave do not apply to probationers for the Staff Corps.

9. The limit of two years laid down for leave will not apply to time spent at the Staff College, which counts as duty.

10. Officers recalled from leave granted under these rules will be given free passage to India, or not, according to the Transport Regulations in force at the time.

11. Advances of pay to officers proceeding on and returning from leave out of India will be granted as heretofore.

12. Extensions of leave, except on medical certificate or in special or urgent cases in which the Secretary of State decides according to circumstances, cannot be granted without the assent of the proper authority in India as defined in Rule V.

13. Extensions of leave beyond one year under the new rules are not claimable in any way as a right,

but are admissible only at the discretion of the proper authority, and then on special and urgent grounds.

14. An Officer subject to these rules, on promotion to Major-General will not be granted leave under them, but, if permitted to reside in England or elsewhere out of India, will be restricted to the English pay of his rank.

15. Officers subject to these rules who are in the following categories, and who entered the Indian Army or Staff Corps before the 1st July 1881, may elect to reside out of India on the allowances admissible under those rules until succession to the Colonel's allowance or promotion to Major-General, whichever may happen first; or to remain in India on the unemployed Indian pay and allowances of their rank if unemployed.

(a.) Officers vacating regimental commands, remaining in the service for the attainment of the highest retirement pension, or the Colonel's allowance.

(b.) Officers over 55 years of age, who may be required under the rules to vacate Staff, Departmental, or Civil appointments, and for whom other suitable employment cannot be found.

(c.) Officers of the Commissariat, Military Accounts, and Judge Advocate-General's Departments, who vacate their appointments on attaining to 52 years of age.

(d.) Officers who on the 8th August 1884 were holding appointments of unlimited tenure to which they were appointed before the 1st January 1884, and who vacate their appointments on completing seven years' tenure from date of appointment or such further extension as may be granted to enable them to complete 32 years' service for pension, not exceeding nine years in all.

(e.) Squadron or Wing Commanders who vacate their appointments on attaining 52 years of age.

(f.) Colonels who entered the Staff Corps after the 12th September 1866 and before the 1st July 1881 and Colonels of the General List, after transfer to the Supernumerary List.

The limit of absence from duty imposed by the new leave rules will not be held applicable to these Officers.

16. Officers who may have entered civil employment after the 30th June, 1856, and before the 1st July, 1887, without having elected the leave rules for the Staff Corps, will be entitled, on reverting to military duty, to elect them under the conditions laid down in the third clause of Rule VII.

17. Officers who may have entered civil employment after the 30th June, 1856, having previously elected the leave rules for the Staff Corps, and officers who may have entered the Staff Corps under those rules and subsequently taken civil employment, will be absolutely under the rules of the Civil Leave Code so long as they remain in civil employment, but on reverting to military duty within ten years of their entry into civil employment, as allowed under G. G. O. No. 135 of 1884, they will again come under the leave rules for the Staff Corps.

18. Officers struck off the effective list of the army under the provisions of G. G. O. No. 135 of 1884, after ten years' absence from military duty, cannot elect or revert to the leave rules for the Staff Corps, except for the purpose of immediate retirement, with the advantage of counting all leave as service for pension, as provided for in Clause 113, India Army Circulars 1886 (para. 1, above).

* I. A. C. Clauses 113 and 197 of 1886, 37, 105, and 106 of 1887, 78 of 1888, and 44 of 1889.
† Army Regulations, India, Vol. I., Part I., Article 1613 Clause (c).

MILITARY FURLOUGH REGULATIONS.

lxxvii

(India Army Circulars, March 1887, Clause 36.)

All officers in military employ, irrespective of rank or the furlough or leave regulations to which they may be subject, who require an extension on private affairs of their furlough or leave out of India, must obtain the previous assent of the authorities in India under whom they may be serving, to apply for such extension.

This permission, when obtained, must be attached to their application to the India Office for the required extension.

ADVANCES OF PAY.

Rules regarding advances of pay to Officers under the Furlough Rules of 1868 and 1875, and the Leave Rules for the Indian Staff Corps, published in Army Circulars, India, 16 April 1886:—

Officers proceeding on furlough to Europe may, if they desire it, receive an advance equivalent to three months' furlough pay.

Officers returning from furlough or from temporary half pay will similarly be granted advances of pay at the following rates, on obtaining permission to return:—

For a Subaltern ... ... ... £65
For a Captain ... ... ... £100
For a Field Officer ... ... ... £150

No advance, however, will be issued to an officer who obtains from the Fund to which he is a subscriber a loan equal to or greater than the advance author-

ized for his rank, and an officer who obtains from his Fund a loan less in amount than such advance will be granted only the difference between the two.

Recovery of the advances made in England to be effected in India by five equal monthly instalments at the rate of exchange fixed for the year in which the advance was made.

The following advances on return to duty will be issue to Warrant Officers, on the same conditions:—

To First Class Warrant Officers ... } £50
To other Warrant Officers with honorary commissions ...
To Second Class Warrant Officers ... £25

ENGLISH FURLOUGH PAY.

INDIAN PENSION REGULATIONS.

STAFF CORPS.

1. Officers who entered the Staff Corps before or on the 12th September 1866, are entitled to pensions and additional pensions at the following rates:—

Ordinary Pension.

Per Annum.
£ s. d.
After 20 years' service for pension ..... 191 12 6
24 " " ..... 292 0 0
28 " " ..... 365 0 0
32 " " ..... 456 5 0
35 " " ..... *600 0 0
38 " " .... *750 0 0

Additional Pension.

Per Annum.
£ s. d.
After 20 years' service from date of 1st commission... 97
21 " " " 105
22 " " " 115
23 " " " 126
24 " " " 138
25 " " " 152
26 " " " 168
27 " " " 186
28 " " " 206
29 " " " 230
30 " " " 257
31 " " " 289
32 " " " 327

2. Officers who entered the Staff Corps after the 12th September 1866, but before the 1st July 1881, are entitled to pensions at the following rates:—

Per Annum.
£ s. d.
After 20 years' service for pension ... .. 250
24 " " .... 365
28 " " .... 500
32 " " .... 700
38 " " .... 750

3. Officers who entered the Staff Corps after the 1st July 1881, may retire on the rates laid down in paragraph 2, with the exception of the pension of £750 for 28 years' service. These Officers are subject to compulsory retirement, in the grades of Lieutenant-Colonel, Colonel, or General Officer, on account of age or continuous non-employment. General Officers thus retired will receive—

£
In any grade, at 62 years of age..... 800 a-year.
Lieutenant-Generals, at 67 years of age 900 "
Generals, at 67 years of age ..... 1,000 "

They may retire voluntarily—

In any grade, at 60, on £750 a-year.
Lieutenant-General, at 65, on £850 a-year,
General, at 65, on £950 a-year,
No General Officer receiving less, on retirement,
than £700 a-year.

4. Officers who entered the Staff Corps before the 1st July, 1881, provided they have relinquished their claim to Colonel's allowance, may claim the benefit of the above pensions for General Officers.

CAVALRY AND INFANTRY.

5. Officers of the local service, that is, under the old conditions of appointment, are entitled to the scales of ordinary and additional pension fixed in paragraph 1 for the Officers who entered the Staff Corps up to the 12th September 1866.

6. Officers of the General Lists, that is, those under the conditions of appointment which came into force in 1859, may retire on the scale given in paragraph 2 for Officers who have entered the Staff Corps after the 12th September 1866.

7. Officers of Cavalry and Infantry, provided they have relinquished their claim to Colonel's allowances, may also claim the benefits of the pensions for General Officers given in paragraph 3.

MEDICAL SERVICE.

8. The scale of pension for Medical Officers is as follows:—

Per Annum. £
After 17 years' service for pension ... .. 292
20 " " .... 365
25 " " .... 500
30 " " .... 700

with extra pensions of £350 and £250 respectively to Officers who have completed a five years' tour of duty as Surgeon-General or Deputy Surgeon-General.

9. A Medical Officer who entered the service before the 13th January, 1860, and who is compulsorily retired on attaining the age of 55 or 58 years, may, however, retire under previous regulations, i.e., on the next higher pension to that to which he would have been entitled on the old scales. The only "next higher" rates which give any pecuniary advantage are those of £458 per annum after 24 years' service for pension, and £550 after 27, 28, or 29 years' service for pension.

10. Four extra pensions of £100 a year each are annually granted to the senior officers of the Indian Medical Service in the proportion of two for the Bengal, and one each for the Madras and Bombay Presidencies. These extra pensions are offered yearly in rotation to Surgeons-General, Deputy Surgeons-General, and to all Brigade Surgeons; but under no circumstances is the extra pension given to a Surgeon-General or Deputy Surgeon-General until he has completed three years' actual service in the grade, excluding all leave except privilege leave, and then only when from failing health or other unforeseen causes he may be unable to complete his full tour of service. Applications for these pensions must be made to the Surgeons-General with the Government of India, Madras, and Bombay respectively, on or before the 1st April in each year. (See Clause 91 of India Army Circulars, 1886.)

VETERINARY DEPARTMENT.

11. The following are the rates of pension for Veterinary Surgeons:—

Per Annum. £
After 20 years' service for pension ... .. 200
25 " " .... 350
30 " " .... 450

HALF PAY.

12. Combatant Officers receive the same rates of half pay as those granted to Officers of the same ranks in the British service.†

13. Medical Officers receive the British rates of half pay of the corresponding military rank.

SERVICE FOR PENSION.

14. Service for pension (except as shown in the following paragraphs) commences from date of arrival in India and includes all leave and furlough taken after that date.

15. Officers who were appointed from Addiscombe count the period passed at the seminary after attaining the age of 16 years.

16. Medical Officers count as service the period passed at the Army Medical School.

17. Officers appointed to the Staff Corps from the British Service are allowed to count service out of India before joining the Staff Corps, to an extent not exceeding two years in any case.

18. Officers who have entered the Staff Corps from the British Service only become entitled to pension after one of the fixed periods specified when not less than one half of the period has been passed in the Staff Corps.

19. Under the Furlough Regulations of 1796 all periods of leave within Indian limits reckon as service, all absence beyond Indian limits being deducted.

20. Under the Furlough Regulations of 1854, or those of 1858, the following periods of furlough and leave reckon as service:—

Combatant Officers.

2 years in 20 years' service.
3 " 24 "
4 " 28 "
5 " 32 "
6 " 38 "

* Additional pensions are not granted with these two pensions.

† As laid down in Royal Warrant of 10th June 1884

Medical Officers and Veterinary Surgeons.
2 years in 17 years' service.
3 " 20 " "
4 " 25 " "
5 " 30 " "

21. Under the Furlough Regulations of 1875, all leave within Indian limits, and all furlough earned by length of service (i.e., one year for every five years' service in India,) reckon as service.

22. Under certain conditions, leave rendered necessary by wounds or by illness solely contracted on field service is allowed to reckon as service.

23. No deduction on account of leave of absence is made from the periods of service for the additional pensions specified in paragraph 1.

COMMUTATION OF PENSION.

24. An Officer retiring under the above regulations will be allowed, under certain conditions,* to commute a portion, not exceeding one-third, of his pension, which commutation may take place on retirement or at any time subsequent thereto.

WARRANT OFFICERS.

(These rules took effect from the 1st Sept., 1881.)

1. All Warrant Officers on the Establishment on 16th September, 1881, are allowed the option of electing the old or the new scale of pensions at any time previous to retirement. The distinction between the invalid-pension, which can be drawn only in India, and the retiring pension, is abolished.

2. The periods of service entitling warrant officers to retiring pensions are as follows:—

  • 24 years' total service in Europe and India, including 16 years in the department,
  • 26 years' total service in Europe and India, including 14 years in the department.
  • 28 years' total service in Europe and India, including 12 years in the department.

3. The retiring pensions are—

England or the Colonies. India. Per annum.
Deputy Commissary ... £216 Rs. 200
Assistant Commissary ... 144 140
Deputy Assistant Commissary ... 108 100
Conductor ... 90 85
Sub-Conductor ... 72 70

4. A warrant officer of any grade, wishing to retire after 21 years' total service, receives a pension of £54 per annum in England or the Colonies, or Rs. 50 per mensem in India.

5. A warrant officer declared by a medical board to be unfit for further service in India, after completion of half the periods of service, army and departmental, laid down in paragraph 3, receives—

* See below.

England or the Colonies. India. Per annum.
Deputy Commissary ... £172 Rs. 160
Assistant Commissary ... 115 110
Deputy Assistant Commissary ... 86 80
Conductor ... 72 68
Sub-Conductor ... 58 56

6. If he has served less than half the prescribed periods, but more than 14 years altogether, he receives—

England or the Colonies. India. Per annum.
Deputy Commissary ... £115 Rs. 110
Assistant Commissary ... 86 80
Deputy Assistant Commissary ... 72 68
Conductor ... 58 56
Sub-Conductor ... 43 40

7. If he has not served 14 years altogether, his case will be specially considered.

8. A warrant officer, whose disqualification for further service in India has been caused by intemperance or irregular habits, receives, if he has served half the periods of service, army and departmental, laid down in paragraph 3—

England or the Colonies. India. Per annum.
Deputy Commissary ... £130 Rs. 120
Assistant Commissary ... 86 85
Deputy Assistant Commissary ... 65 60
Conductor ... 54 50
Sub-Conductor ... 43 42

9. If he has served less than half the prescribed periods, but more than 14 years altogether, he receives—

England or the Colonies. India. Per annum.
Deputy Commissary ... £86 Rs. 85
Assistant Commissary ... 65 60
Deputy Assistant Commissary ... 54 50
Conductor ... 43 42
Sub-Conductor ... 32 30

10. If he has not served 14 years altogether, his case will be specially considered.

11. Retirement is compulsory at the age of 55 years; and all warrant officers of that age in the service on 16th September, 1881, will be required to retire at the expiration of one year from that date.

12. All warrant officers on the establishment on 16th September, 1881, will be allowed the option of electing the old or the new scale of pensions at any time previous to retirement.

13. Pension, according to the present scale and conditions, is admissible to the widow of a warrant officer who may die, either while in the service or on the Pension establishment, provided, in the latter case, that she married the deceased warrant officer previous to his retiring from the army or being invalided.

REGULATIONS FOR THE COMMUTATION OF PENSIONS OF OFFICERS OF THE INDIAN SERVICE.

1. These Regulations shall be applicable only to Officers retired on or after the 1st July, 1881.

2. An Officer may be allowed to commute a portion, not exceeding one-third, of his pension, which commutation may take place on retirement, or at any time subsequent thereto.

3. An Officer in Europe desirous of commuting must make application, in writing, to the Under Secretary of State for India, stating his desire so to commute, and furnishing information as to his age, rank, and the amount of his pension.

4. If the Secretary of State for India in Council shall approve the commutation, the Officer will be so informed, and his application will be forwarded to the Standing Medical Board at the India Office.

5. The Board will require the Officer to give such information as to his health and habits as is usual in

the case of persons proposing to assure their lives in assurance companies, and he will be required to submit himself for personal examination by the Board at the India Office.

6. An Officer in India desirous of commuting must address the authorities of his Presidency. He will be required to go through similar forms, Boards, which will, when practicable, be presided over by the chief Civil Medical Administrative Officer of the Government, being formed in the Presidency towns of Calcutta, Madras, and Bombay, under orders of the respective Governments.

7. The basis of commutation, subject to an addition of years of age in the case of impaired lives, shall be the following table, the age of the Officer being reckoned at the age he will attain on the birthday next succeeding the date upon which the capitalised sum will become payable:—

Age next Birthday. Number of Years' Purchase. Present Value of the Annuity of £100. Age next Birthday. Number of Years' Purchase. Present Value of the Annuity of £100.
£ s. d. £ s. d.
2115.002581,500 5 25111.213561,121 7 1
2214.961111,496 2 35210.981451,098 2 11

3613.914781,391 9 7667.62437762 8 9
3713.790161,379 0 4677.37082737 1 8

3913.527381,352 14 9696.86850686 17 0
4013.388541,338 17 1706.62170662 3 5
4113.243131,324 6 3716.38068635 1 4
4213.085681,308 11 4726.14448614 8 11
4312.920501,292 1 0735.90561590 11 3
4412.744831,274 9 8745.65540565 10 10
4512.558341,255 16 8755.39358539 10 9
4612.360961,236 1 11765.12566512 11 4
4712.146901,214 13 10774.85132481 10 8
4811.923391,192 6 9784.50565450 11 3
4911.691281,169 2 7794.20762420 15 3
5011.453621,145 7 3803.91477391 9 6

8. The commutation having been sanctioned, the applicant will be informed, and will have the option of withdrawing his application within 30 days after receiving that notice.

9. If the applicant shall not withdraw his application within that period, the decision will be considered final, and the commutation of pension will take place absolutely.

10. In every case the capitalised sum will be payable in India, the sterling amount being converted at the official rate of exchange for the year in which the payment is made.

11. In the case of an officer in Europe, the capitalised sum will be payable at the expiration of two months, and in the case of an officer in India, at the

expiration of one month from the date of the commutation being sanctioned. In each case the officer's pension will be issued in full up to the date of payment, unless any delay has been caused by the officer's own fault or that of his agents.

12. In any case in which a pensioner shall accept public employment, involving the withholding of pension, a deduction will be made from the salary payable to him equal to the amount which would have been abated from the pension if it had not been commuted, and in the event of his becoming entitled to a pension in respect of such employment, the amount of such pension granted to him shall not exceed the amount which he might have received had his pension not been so commuted.

CONDITIONS OF TRANSFER TO TEMPORARY HALF-PAY.

COMMISSIONED OFFICERS.

(Sanctioned by Despatch to Government of India, No. 432, dated 22nd December, 1881.)

Officers placed on temporary half-pay, at the recommendation of the Medical Board of the India Office, shall be examined by the same Board as to their fitness to return to duty every two years, and if still reported unfit at the third examination, that is, after being six years on the temporary half-pay list, they shall be transferred to the retired list on permanent half-pay. An Officer reported to be unfit at the commencement of the second period, i.e., after two years on half-pay, may, however, be permitted voluntarily to retire on permanent half-pay.

Officers unable to appear before the Medical Board at the stated times will be required to furnish a certificate, signed by two medical men, as to their inability. An Officer who is continuously unable to appear will be retired permanently after six years without further report.

Officers who, in view to being restored to the active list, may wish to appear before the Board at other times than those specified, may be permitted to do so. Officers ordered periodically to appear before the Board will receive their travelling expenses and lodging allowance on the Home scale, but those appearing at other times, at their own wish, must do so at their own expense.

WARRANT OFFICERS.

(Sanctioned by Despatch to Government of India, No. 25, dated 24th January 1884.)

A Warrant Officer transferred to temporary half-pay may be permitted to remain on half-pay till his total continuous absence from duty, on furlough and half-pay together, amounts to four years. If then still unfit to return to duty, he must be pensioned on the rate of pension for which he had qualified at the time of being placed on half-pay.

No period spent on half-pay will count as service for Indian pension, nor will a Warrant Officer be eligible for promotion while he is on half-pay. A Warrant Officer replaced on the effective list, on return to duty from half-pay, will be re-employed at once, being borne as a supernumerary in his grade until absorbed by the next vacancy.

Warrant Officers will be required to appear before a Medical Board at the expiration of one year on half-pay, and again, if retained on the half-pay list, at the end of the second year; and those who, in view to being restored to the effective list, may wish to appear before the Board at other times than those specified, may be permitted to do so. Warrant Officers ordered periodically to appear before the Board will be allowed, in this country, their bonâ fide travelling expenses, and lodging allowance at the rates stated below, but those appearing at other times, and at their own wish, must do so at their own expense.

Rates of Lodging Allowance.

Deputy Commissary ... ... ... ... ... } 2s. 3d. per diem.
Senior Apothecary after 3 years' service in the 1st grade ... ... ... ... ...
Assistant Commissary ... ... ... ... ... } 2s. "
Deputy Assistant Commissary ... ... ... ... ...
Senior Apothecary under 3 years' service in the 1st grade ... ... ... ... ... } 1s. 4d. "
Conductor ... ... ... ... ...
Sub-Conductor ... ... ... ... ...
Medical Warrant Officers below the rank of Senior Apothecary

ISSUE OF FURLOUGH AND RETIRED PAY.

Military Furlough and Retired Pay is made up at the India Office to the 31st January, 30th April, 31st July, and 31st October, and is payable on the 1st of the following month.

EXTRACT FROM REGULATIONS OF THE MILITARY FUND LATELY CALLED
LORD CLIVE'S.

WIDOWS' PENSIONS.

1. Officers' widows will be required to sign a declaration to the effect that their husbands did not die possessed of real and personal property to the following amounts, and that they themselves do not, with what their husbands left and from all other sources, possess, if the widow of a colonel, £4,000; a lieut.-colonel, £3,000; a major, £2,500; a captain, £2,000; a lieutenant £1,000.

2. The rates of pension are as follows:—

RANKS. Widows' Pensions.
Per dm. Per annum.
s. d. £ s. d.
Colonel, Surgeon-General, and Deputy Surgeon-General 6 3 114 1 3
Lieut.-Colonel, Brigade-Surgeon, and Surgeon-Major after 20 years' service 5 0 91 5 0
Major, Surgeon-Major, and Chaplain of 15 years' service 3 9 68 8 9
Captain, Surgeon, Chaplain of 7 years' service, and Veterinary Surgeon of 20 years' service 2 6 45 12 6
Chaplain under 7 years' service 1 9 31 18 9
Lieutenant and Veterinary Surgeon 1 3 22 16 3

3. The pensions continue only during widowhood.

4. Pensions lapsing on re-marriage are not restored on a second widowhood.

5. Pensioners neglecting to claim the pension for three half-years will be considered as dead, and no arrears for a longer period than two years back from the date of application for admission or re-admission, as the case may be, will be allowed either to claimants or to pensioners after admission.

6. The widows of officers formerly of the Indian service, who have joined the British army, retain their claims to pension from this Fund.

7. The widows of officers who have retired from the service are not eligible to the benefits of this Fund.*

8. The widows of officers who at the time of their death were pensioners on Lord Clive's Fund are entitled to the benefits of the Fund, provided they were married to their husbands prior to the latter having become pensioners on such Fund.

9. These pensions are payable quarterly, on the 1st January, 1st April, 1st July, and 1st October.

INDIA OFFICE, March 1883.

Note.—Applications for admission to this Fund should be addressed to
The Military Secretary, India Office, London, S.W.

INDIAN MARINE.

All first appointments of Officers and Engineers in the Indian Marine are made by the Secretary of State for India in Council.

The limits of age for appointment to the junior executive rank, that of Third Grade Officer, are 17 and 22 years, and no candidate will be appointed who does not possess a Board of Trade certificate as Second Mate.

Applications for appointment can be recorded at the India Office, but will not be submitted for the orders of the Secretary of State until the applicant is duly qualified, and there is a vacancy.

When he is qualified, the Board of Trade Certificate, together with certificates of birth and character, should be transmitted to the Military Secretary, India Office.

* Except in the case of the widows of General Officers compulsorily retired under the provisions of the Royal Warrants of 31st December 1877, 1st May 1878, 25th June 1881, 11th March 1882, and 10th June 1884.

Candidates must pass an examination by the India Office Medical Board prior to appointment.
On appointment a first-class passage to Bombay will be provided, and an advance of pay not exceeding £25, recoverable in India, will be made in aid of outfit.
Pay commences from date of embarkation for India. Service for pension reckons from date of arrival in India.
The names of candidates will be removed from the list on their attaining the age of 23 years.
Any further information relative to the appointment of executive Officers may be obtained on application to the Military Secretary, India Office.
Information in regard to the appointment of Assistant Engineer may be obtained from the Director General of Stores, India Office.
INDIA OFFICE,
June, 1887.

FURLOUGH AND LEAVE RULES OF THE INDIAN MARINE.
MARINE CIRCULAR OF THE GOVERNMENT OF INDIA, NO. 16 OF 1884.

Furlough.

  1. I.—Officers after five years' service in India or under the Government of India may receive one year's furlough, and an additional year for each subsequent five years' service. Furlough not taken at the time it becomes due is not lost, and can, subject to these regulations, be taken later.
  2. II.—An officer may not be absent from duty on any grounds for more than three years consecutively, nor on return from furlough may he receive, except under Rule VII., any further furlough until after an interval of three years.
  3. III.—† Furlough may be spent anywhere, and, when it has been earned by length of service, counts as service for pension. Furlough taken before it is earned by length of service reckons against service for pension till paid off.
  4. IV.—An officer on furlough receives half the pay of his rank, or, if holding a shore appointment, half the pay of his appointment, excluding command, charge, acting, personal, or house allowance, computed according to a rate of exchange to be fixed in each year by the Secretary of State. An officer promoted while on furlough will be entitled to a moiety of the increased pay due to that promotion.
  5. V.—‡ An officer proceeding on or returning from furlough may be granted an advance of three months' furlough pay; and he may further†† be granted the advance in aid of passage money specified in clause IX., if he does not obtain passage by troopship. Advances made in England will be recovered in India by monthly instalments of one-fourth of the pay drawn by the officers. Advances made in India will be noted on the last pay certificates of officers, and no issue of furlough pay will be made till after the sum advanced has accrued as furlough pay.
  6. VI.—Furlough pay may not be drawn for more than two years consecutively.
  7. VII.—Upon certificate of a medical board, an officer not entitled to furlough, who has served not less than one year in India, may be granted furlough. Such furlough will not count as service for pension until it has been paid off by subsequent service under the terms of Rule I. Furlough cannot be anticipated beyond two years in the aggregate, nor for a second term, unless that previously anticipated has been subsequently paid off under the terms of Rule I.
  8. VIII.—An officer who has exhausted the furlough obtainable under these rules, and who may be certified by a medical board to be still unfit for duty, will be retired on the pension or gratuity to which he may be entitled; if not entitled to either pension or gratuity, his case will be specially considered (but see under heading "Temporary Retirement," on p. lxxxvi).
  9. IX.—** Marine officers not holding shore appointments of Rs. 1,000 per mensem will be eligible for the grant of non-regulation passages in troopships when such passages are available. Officers who do not obtain passage in troopships will be allowed advances†† in aid of passage-money, in addition to the advances admissible under Clause V., provided that no officer shall in any case draw advances exceeding three months' pay in all.
  10. X.—Furlough, whether on private affairs or on medical certificate, is restricted to a maximum period of one year in the first instance, and to subsequent extensions not exceeding six months at a time.

General Leave

  1. XI.—General leave may be granted for any period not exceeding six months at a time, but cannot be renewed, except for very urgent reasons, till after two years have elapsed from its expiration. It may be spent anywhere, does not involve forfeiture of appointment and counts as service for pension. It will be granted or refused entirely at the discretion of the Director of the Indian Marine. It may be granted up to a maximum limit of 30 days as subsidiary to furlough, whether on proceeding on furlough or on return from furlough. Leave granted as subsidiary to furlough can only be spent in India.
  2. XII.—During general leave an officer will be restricted to the pay of his grade,* and will forfeit all allowances, which will be paid to his locum tenens. If an officer exceeds the six months' leave admissible at one time under this rule, the entire period will be converted into furlough, and his allowances adjusted accordingly.

Privilege Leave.

  1. XIII.—Subject to the exigencies of the service, Marine officers may be allowed 60 days' privilege leave during each year, and such leave may be taken in instalments.
  2. XIV.—An officer who has privilege leave to his credit when proceeding on leave or furlough does not forfeit it, but privilege leave cannot be taken in continuation of furlough, nor can furlough be obtained in continuation of privilege leave. Privilege leave may, however, be converted into general leave with the sanction of the Director of the Indian Marine, or into furlough with the sanction of the Government of India.
  3. XV.—Privilege leave will be granted by the Director of the Indian Marine, but it will be granted only on the understanding that no additional expense is thereby caused to the State either directly or indirectly, that the applicant's services can be spared, and that satisfactory arrangements for the performance of his duties during his absence can be made.
  4. XVI.—Officers are at liberty to proceed wherever they may desire when on privilege leave, provided they can ensure their return before the expiration of the leave. If an officer exceeds his privilege leave, the whole period of his absence will be converted into general leave or furlough, and his allowances and statement of service for furlough adjusted accordingly.

* This rule will also hold good in the case of an officer holding a shore appointment, in which case the balance will be considered a staff allowance payable to the locum tenens, provided the latter does not thereby draw more than the pay of the appointment.

† As modified by Marine Circular No. 6 of 1885.

‡ As modified by Marine Circular No. 7 of 1886.

** As modified by Marine Circular No. 31 of 1885.

†† Unless holding shore appointments of Rs. 1,000 per mensem. Such officers are ineligible for passage by troopship (cl. ix.) and are also ineligible for any advance exceeding three months' furlough pay (Marine Circular No. 1 of 1888).

Temporary Leave.

XVII.—Temporary leave not exceeding 10 days, and not in anticipation or in extension of any other kind of leave or furlough, may be granted by the Director and Deputy Director of the Indian Marine.

XVIII.—Temporary leave not exceeding 72 hours may be granted by commanders of vessels and heads of departments to officers under their command.

General Regulations.

XIX.—It must be distinctly understood that furlough or leave cannot be claimed as a right. Both are subject to the exigencies of the public service, and will be granted only when an officer's services can be spared. Commanders of vessels and heads of departments will not forward an application for furlough or leave unless they are able to satisfy the Director of the Indian Marine that the officer's services can be spared without any inconvenience to the public service.

XX.—No grant of leave given under the conditions of these rules will count against an officer's service for pension, and leave may be spent anywhere, i.e., within possible limits of return before it expires.

XXI.—Leave is reckoned from the day on which an officer avails himself of it, and it expires on the day he rejoins.

Furlough reckons from date of leaving India or from date of leaving his ship if at an outstation, unless specially stated by authority to commence on an earlier date; and in the event of an officer returning to India before the expiration of his furlough, it ceases on the date of his return.

The day of return, whether from leave or from furlough, is a day of duty.

XXII.—Officers on furlough will draw their pay from the India Office, but officers on general or privilege leave must arrange to draw their pay in India.

XXIII.—As a rule, leave will be granted only during the period of the south-west monsoon.

XXIV.—Officers proceeding on furlough will report their departure personally at the office of the Director or Deputy Director of the Indian Marine, except when they avail themselves of it at an outstation. An officer returning from furlough or leave will rejoin the ship to which he belongs; but if this is impracticable, and he has not received instructions (which he should apply for beforehand), he will rejoin at Bombay or Calcutta, reporting himself in person to the Director or Deputy Director. On the departure of an officer on furlough or leave, or on the return of an officer therefrom, the commander of the vessel will cause an entry to be made of the date in the ship's log book, and will forward an extract thereof by the next post to the Director.

XXV.—Officers arriving in Europe on furlough or on general leave* must report their arrival in writing to the Military Secretary at the India Office, and state their address; and for extension of furlough or for permission to return to duty, they must also apply to the Military Secretary. † Before applying for extension of furlough on private affairs, they must obtain permission to do so from the Government of India, through the Director of Marine. The permission thus obtained must be attached to the application for extension, and submitted to the Military Secretary at the India Office.

Officers proceeding to the Colonies on furlough should apply to India for extensions of furlough and for permission to return to duty.

XXVI.—During the absence of an officer on leave or furlough, he must keep the Director of the Indian Marine acquainted with his actual address, and be prepared to return immediately to India, should his services be required. When on leave, such recall order will be issued by the Director; when on furlough in Europe, by the Secretary of State. Officers spending their furlough in Europe must keep the Secretary of State informed of their address.

XXVII.—Applications for leave or furlough must be made on the authorized form, which is obtainable from the Director or Deputy Director of the Indian Marine.

NOTE.—(Marine Circular No. 10 of 1855.)

The above rules are applicable to—

  1. I.—Officers on the Graded and supernumerary List of Executive Officers
  2. II.—Officers on the Graded List of Engineers.
  3. III.—Gazetted clerks.
  4. IV.—Warrant officers who may be specially admitted to the Rules.

* Marine Circular No. 19 of 1888.

† Marine Circular No. 3 of 1889.

ESTABLISHMENT OF OFFICERS OF THE INDIAN MARINE AND RATES OF PAY AND ALLOWANCES.

MARINE CIRCULARS OF THE GOVERNMENT OF INDIA, Nos. 26 OF 1883 and 4 OF 1886.

25 Commanders . { 7 1st Grade on 500 per mensem.
{ 9 2nd " " on 400 " "
{ 9 3rd " " on 350 " "
{ Over 6 years' seniority, 250 " "
32 First Grade Officers { " 3 " " 200 " "
{ Under 3 " " 150 " "
19 Second Grade Officers . . . 125 " "
13 Third Grade Officers . . . 100 " "
Total 59 officers.
Command Allowances.

For the command of a sea-going ship in commission (300—2,000 tons gross), from Rs. 2 to Rs. 10 per diem. Half rates, for command of a harbour ship in commission, or a sea-going ship in reserve or out of commission.

Charge Allowances.

For the senior officer of a vessel, Rs. 1 to Rs. 2 per diem, in addition to pay.

PENSION REGULATIONS OF THE INDIAN MARINE.

MARINE CIRCULARS OF THE GOVERNMENT OF INDIA, Nos. 13 OF 1885 and 14 OF 1887.

1. The following is the scale of pensions and compassionate allowances admissible to Officers and Engineers of Her Majesty's Indian Marine and to their families.

2. The service for pension of an officer of the Marine reckons from date of joining his ship or appointment if he is 19 years of age or over; otherwise from date of attaining that age.

3. An officer admitted to the service after 25 years of age will be subject to reduction of pension in the manner and under the rules laid down in the Civil Pension Code.

4. Retirement at the age of 55 years will be compulsory, and all Officers and Engineers of that age now in the service, as well as those who may attain that age within one year, will be required to retire at the expiration of one year from the date of this order.* Should an officer holding the appointment of Director or Deputy Director of the Indian Marine attain that age during his incumbency, he may, with the approval of the Government of India, complete five years in the appointment.

[This rule will not be applicable to those officers who have already received a special extension of tenure.]

5. The following is the scale of pensions:—

Designation Officers invalided after 3 and under 15 years' service owing to disease contracted in and by the service. After 15 years' service if invalided, and 20 years without invaliding. After 25 years' service if invalided. After 30 years' service, inclusive of the periods of furlough allowed to count as service for pension.
After 3 years' service. After 6 years' service. After 10 years' service.
Per Diem. Per mensem.
Rs. A. P. Rs. A. P. Rs. A. P. Rs. Rs. Rs.
Commanders, 1st grade ... .. 2 8 0 4 0 0 5 8 0 270 410 490
Commanders, 2nd grade ... .. 220 330 400
Commanders, 3rd grade ... .. 190 280 340
First Grade Officers of six years' seniority. 2 4 0 2 8 0 4 0 0 140 210 260
First Grade Officers of three years' seniority. 120 170 210
Second Grade Officers ... .. 1 12 0 2 4 0 2 8 0 90 130 160
Third Grade Officer ... .. 80 120 150
Chief Engineers on Rs. 400 per mensem. 70 110 130
Chief Engineers on Rs. 300 per mensem. 2 8 0 4 0 0 5 8 0 210 320 380
Engineers of eight years' service in that grade. 180 270 320
Engineers of four years' service in that grade. 2 4 0 2 8 0 4 0 0 150 23 250
Engineers under four years' service in that grade. 140 200 250
Assistant Engineers ... .. 1 12 0 2 4 0 2 8 0 130 190 230
100 160 190

* The pension in no case to exceed one-third of the emoluments drawn by the officer when invalided. The periods of service mentioned in the foregoing table are inclusive of the periods of furlough allowed to count towards pension.

6. The following extra pension will be granted to an officer of the Indian Marine retiring after five years service as Deputy Director or Director:—

For five years' continuous service as Director Per annum.
For five years' continuous service as Deputy Director Rs. 2,000
For five years' continuous service, partly as Deputy Director and partly as Director, of which not less than two years shall have been as Director Rs. 1,000
Rs. 1,500

7. Service in the survey, or in port or dockyard appointments, gives no claim to any higher pension than that fixed for an officer's rank in the Marine. Officers not on the Marine list now serving in such appointments will remain under their present rules.

8. The pensions of warrant officers, gunners, and clerks will be calculated under the rules for the Uncovenanted Service, as laid down in the Civil Pension Code, Rs. 60 per mensem (compensation in lieu of board and lodging) being added to their pay for the calculation of pension.

9. Pensions will be payable in rupees in India. If paid in England, they will be converted at the rate of exchange fixed yearly for the adjustment of financial transactions between the Imperial and Indian Governments.

10. An officer may be allowed to commute a portion, not exceeding one-third of his pension, the commuted value being payable in India under the rules in clause 23, India Army Circulars of 1884.†

* The amount of superannuation pension is that which would be admissible as an invalid pension. (Marine Circular No. 5 of 1885.)

† See page lxxix.

11. Compassionate allowances on the following scale will be granted to the families of officers who may be drowned or meet with a violent death in the execution of duty:—

Widow. Child.
Commander ... .. £100 a year, or Rs. 100 a month. £20 a year, or Rs. 20 a month.
Port Officer ... ..
Chief Engineer ... ..
First Grade Officer ... .. £75 a year, or Rs. 75 a month. £15 a year, or Rs. 15 a month.
Engineer ... ..
Second Grade Officer ... ..
Third Grade Officer ... .. £50 a year, or Rs. 50 a month. £10 a year, or Rs. 10 a month.
Assistant Engineer ... ..

The cases of the families of the Director or Deputy Director will be specially considered.

12. The regulations and conditions under which all such compassionate allowances are to be awarded will be analogous to those prescribed for the military service.

Officers are required to signify their intention to accept the above pension rules by the 31st December, 1885, otherwise it will be understood that they have decided to remain under the rules now applicable to them.

TEMPORARY RETIREMENT—INDIAN MARINE.

(Marine Circular, No. 15 of 1887.)

1. Officers of the Indian Marine, who after exhausting the furlough admissible to them under the Rules printed on p. lxxxiii. are unfit for service, but not irremedially broken in health, may be temporarily transferred to the Retired List.

2. An officer granted temporary retirement will be permitted to remain in that position until his total continuous absence from duty, on furlough and temporary retirement together, amounts to four years. If then still unfit to return to duty, he must be placed on the Permanent Retired List, receiving the pension, if any, or gratuity for which he had qualified at the time of being granted temporary retirement.

3. Time spent on temporary retirement will not count as service for pension, nor will an officer be eligible for promotion while he is in that position. An officer replaced on the effective list from temporary retirement will be restored to the grade, and numerical position in that grade, which he occupied before his retirement.

4. Officers will be required to appear before a Medical Board at the expiration of one year on temporary retirement, and again, if retained in that position, before the end of the second year: on doing so they will be allowed their bona fide travelling expenses, and lodging allowance at from 3s. to 5s. per diem, according to rank. In case of appearing before a Board at other times than those specified, at their own request, they must do so at their own expense.

5. Temporary retirement will be subject to the condition that the disease which necessitates it must have been contracted in and by the service: and it will also be subject to Rule 3 of the Pension Regulations of the Indian Marine (p. lxxv).

INDIAN MILITARY SERVICE FAMILY PENSION REGULATIONS.

(Instituted 1st Jan. 1873.)

REGULATIONS FOR THE PROVISION OF PENSIONS FOR THE WIDOWS AND ORPHANS OF MILITARY, MEDICAL, AND ECCLESIASTICAL OFFICERS IN THE SERVICE OF THE GOVERNMENT OF INDIA.

1. Pensions will be provided under these regulations for the widows and orphans of the following officers on the Indian establishment, not being entitled to the benefits of any of the late Military and Medical Funds:

Officers of the Staff Corps.
Officers of the Indian Medical Department.
Chaplains.

3. Every European officer who enters the service on and after the 1st January 1873, must, as a condition of his appointment, provide pensions for his family under these regulations. A Native Commissioned Medical Officer will be required to declare his intention of joining the Fund or not within three months from the date of his taking up his appointment in India.

Note—On appointment to the Service, Medical Officers and Chaplains are admitted to the Fund “from date of arrival in India.”

5. For the purposes of these regulations officers are classed as follows:—

Class I.—Lieutenant-Colonels in receipt of Colonel's allowances, and Surgeons-General.
„ II.—Lieutenant-Colonels, Archdeacons, Deputy Surgeons-General, Brigade Surgeons, and Surgeons-Major of 20 years' service.
„ III.—Majors, Senior Chaplains, and Surgeons-Major.
„ IV.—Captains, Junior Chaplains, and Surgeons who have served the Government of India for more than six years.
„ V.—Lieutenants and Surgeons who have served the Government of India for less than six years.

6. The pensions provided under these regulations are payable monthly in India, and quarterly in arrears in England, and to date of death.

7. The amounts of the pensions provided are as follows, in addition to any pensions or compassionate allowances granted under Royal Warrant.

(N.B.—The one now in force is dated 15th November 1887.)

TABLE I.—Pension to a Widow during Widowhood, according to the Class of her Husband at the time of his death.

Amount of pension to widow per ann.: CLASS OF HUSBAND AT DEATH.
I. II. III. IV. V.
In Europe ..... £ 160 £ 130 £ 100 £ 70 £ 40
Rs. Rs. Rs. Rs. Rs.
In India ..... 1,669 1,356 1,043 730 417

TABLE II.—Pensions to the Orphan Children of all Classes alike.

From birth till age of 6 years. From age of 6 till age of 12 years. From age of 12 till age of 21 years. To females only from age of 21 years for life, or till marriage.
Amount of pension to each orphan: £ £ £ £
In Europe ..... 10 20 30 45
Rs. Rs. Rs. Rs.
In India ..... 104 208 313 469

8. The pension of a minor orphan will be paid to his or her mother, or other duly appointed or recognized guardian.

NOTE.—Annuitants in India must draw their pensions in Indian currency.

Contributions.

9. The contributions for pensions under these regulations, and the pensions of widows, will be regulated by the class of the contributing officer. An officer cannot secure a higher pension for his widow by contributing as if he belonged to a higher class.

* As an exception to this rule, an officer who has completed five years service as a substantive Lieutenant-Colonel has the option of subscribing in Class I. (A. C. 34, of Jan. 1881.)

10. Contributions will be deducted by paymasters or other disbursing officers from the pay abstracts of officers contributing.

10A. The circumstance that an Officer is not in receipt of pay does not relieve him from the necessity of paying his contributions.

11. Donations may be paid in one sum, or in 12 equal monthly instalments. If a donation be paid in instalments, interest on the unpaid portion will be levied at the rate of 4½ per cent. per annum. The balance of any contribution due at an officer's death will be recovered rateably, without interest, from the pensions of his widow and orphans, in 12 monthly instalments in India, or four quarterly instalments in England.

11A. Contributions which become due from subscribers when in Europe must be paid in England in sterling. If a subscriber, previous to quittingting India, has commenced the payment of any donation by instalments, he may either complete the payment of the donation in India before leaving, or pay the remaining instalments in England at English rates; but an Officer on half-pay who continues to subscribe at Indian rates has the option of paying in India.

12. The following are the rates of donations and monthly contributions prescribed for the present:—

TABLE III.—Donations.

Class of Contributors: On entering the Service of the Government of India married, or on marriage, or re-marriage. On promotion to a higher class.
In India. In Europe. In India. In Europe.
Class I. .... Rs. 4320 £ 384 Rs. 810 £ 72
" II. .... 2160 192 405 36
" III. .... 1080 96 270 24
" IV. .... 540 48 135 12
" V. .... 270 24 — —

Disparity of Age.

If the age of the husband exceed that of the wife by 12 years or more, then the marriage donation shall be regulated by a disparity table (TABLE IV.), which can be obtained from the Director of Funds, India Office.

TABLE V.—Donation, payable by all Classes alike, on the Birth of a Child and, on entering the Service of the Government of India, for each Child then living.

In India. In Europe.
For a son ..... Rs. 168 £ 15
For a daughter ..... 270 24
Between the ages of Extra donation All Officers who, on admission as subscribers, bring on liabilities for daughters above 1 year, are required to pay the extra donation specified in the margin for daughters according to their age at the date of their admission to the Fund.
1 and 2 — 50 Rs.
2 " 3 — 75 "
3 " 4 — 92 "
4 " 5 — 104 "
5 " 6 — 112 "
6 " 7 — 115 "
7 " 8 — 117 "
8 " 9 — 119 "
9 " 10 — 119 "
10 " 11 — 120 "
11 " 12 — 120 "

[G. G. O., No. 728 of 1875.]
(A.C.I. 1880 Cl. 132.)

TABLE VI.—Monthly Contribution by every Officer according to his Class.
Class of Contributor. By each married officer. By each officer who is unmarried or a widower.
In India. In Europe. In India. In Europe.
Rs. A. £ s. Rs. A. £ s. d.
Class I..... 54 0 2 10 27 0 1 4 0
" II..... 43 3 2 0 17 4 0 15 4
" III..... 32 6 1 10 12 15 0 11 6
" IV..... 21 10 1 0 7 9 0 6 8
" V..... 10 13 0 10 4 5 0 3 10

NOTE.—The Europe rates of subscription are payable after retirement and during furlough out of India, and whenever full Indian pay is not received. But an officer on furlough in India, and not in receipt of full Indian pay, will be required to pay only half the Indian rate of contribution. (A. C. I. 1886. Cl. 9.)

TABLE VII.—Additional Monthly Contribution, payable by all Classes alike, for each Child living on the First Day of the Month.
In India. In Europe.
Rs. A. P. £ s. d.
For a son, until age of 21 ..... 1 1 0 0 2 0
For a daughter, until marriage..... 2 11 0 0 5 0

Arrears.

13. Interest at the rate of 4½ per cent. per annum will be charged on arrears. Donations will become due from the date on which the liability is incurred, viz., on marriage, on promotion, or on the birth of a child, but interest will not be charged on sums paid within one month from the date on which they become due.

Reports of Marriages, Births, and Deaths.

14. An officer who is married at the date of his admission into the service must report the fact of his marriage at the time, and the names and dates of birth of his children, if any have been born to him. An officer in the service must report his marriage, or the birth of a child born in wedlock, or the death of a wife or child, within one month of the occurrence, to the Examiner of Fund Accounts in the Presidency, or the Director of Funds, India Office, when an officer is in Europe. Forms for this purpose are given in the Appendix.

15. The birth of every child born alive must be reported; but if a child die before it is thirty days old, no payment in respect of it will be required.

15A. If the report of a marriage, or of the birth of a child, be not made within one month of the occurrence, the donation payable in each case will be increased 2½ per cent., and interest thereon charged from the date of marriage or birth of the child. After six months' delay, donations will be increased 5 per cent., and interest charged in the usual manner. (G.O.G.G. No. 381 of 1877.)

15B. In the event of the report of a marriage or the birth of a child not being made by a subscriber during his lifetime, in accordance with the rules, the widow or child will not be entitled to any benefits. (Army Circulars, India, 1880, Clause II.)

Posthumous Child.

16. A pension will be granted to a posthumous child on payment of the donation prescribed in Table V., which may be recovered by 12 monthly instalments in India, or four quarterly instalments in England. The report of the birth of a posthumous child must be made in Form D., the last sentence being omitted.

Commutation of Contributions.

17. An officer who desires to complete his payments during the term of his service in India, so as to be exempt from contributions after his retirement, will be permitted to do so at rates to be determined by the tables appended to General Hannynngton's report, dated London, 7th June, 1870, or by any other tables that may be hereafter substituted by the Secretary of State for India in Council.

18. An officer may at any time commute his future payments into a single payment, the amount of such payment being regulated by the same tables.

Duration of Pensions.

19. If a widow remarry, her pension will cease during coverture, but if she again become a widow she will be entitled to revert to her pension as widow of her first husband, or, if her last husband have been a contributor, to receive a pension at her option as the widow of her last husband. A widow can only receive one pension.

20. The pension of a male orphan is payable until he attains the age of 21 years.

21. The mother or guardian of a male orphan who has completed the age of 16 may, at any time thereafter (on application in Forms F. to H. annexed to these regulations), have the residue of his pension commuted into a single payment, for the due application of which the mother or guardian will be held responsible. The following table gives the commuted values of such pensions:—

TABLE VIII.—Showing the present Value of the Pension of a Male Orphan, according to his Age.
Years. 16 17 18 19 20
Months. £ £ £ £ £
0 129 106 81 56 28
3 123 100 75 49 21
6 117 94 68 42 14
9 111 87 62 35 7

22. The pension of a female orphan continues until her marriage, and then ceases absolutely. It does not revive on widowhood.

Good Conduct.

23. The pensions provided under these regulations shall, in all cases, be held during good behaviour only, and will be withheld if any pensioner is found to be leading an immoral life, or on proof of any gross misconduct. The order of the Secretary of State for India in Council in any such case shall be final and conclusive.

Alienation of Pension.

24. A pension paid under these regulations may not be transferred or assigned, or in any way alienated, from the pensioner to whom or for whose benefit it is payable.

Passage Money.

25. An officer may, by a special contribution, provide passage money from India for his widow and orphans. The amount so secured will belong to the officer's estate, but will be primarily applicable to the provision of passage money and outfit for his widow and children. The amount secured must not exceed 1,500 rupees, and a health certificate must in every case be furnished by the subscriber. The value will be determined by the Tables in Major-General Hannington's report.

25a. The special contribution can be paid by monthly instalments not exceeding 24, the instalments in arrear bearing interest at \frac{4}{1} per cent. per annum, and in the event of a subscriber dying before completion of the payment, the balance due at his death will be deducted from the sum insured.

25b. Native commissioned medical officers are required to pay, as in the case of their ordinary contributions, 5 per cent. over and above the ordinary rates of special contribution (Army Circulars India, 1884, Clause 33).

25c. In the event of a subscriber's family not having occasion to make use of the passage money for which special contribution has been made, it will be paid to his estate on his death, irrespective of whether this occurs before or after his retirement, or whether he continued to subscribe to the pension branch up to the date of his death.

If an officer on retirement desires to sever his connection with the Insurance Branch, he will be allowed the option of receiving the value of the sum assured at the date of withdrawal; in which case the value of the sum assured will be determined by the tables in the Regulations according to the member's age; e.g., a member withdrawing at the age of thirty, who has paid the special contribution to secure a sum of Rs. 1,500, will be entitled to receive on withdrawal:—Rs. 1,500 \times .391574 \times 1.022 = \text{Rs. } 600.

If, however, the option is declined at the date of withdrawal from the pension branch, the sum originally assured will be paid in accordance with the rules at the decease of the subscriber. (A. C. I. 1886. Cl. 85.)

Transfer to Her Majesty's Line Regiments, and Officers who resign.

26. Officers who may be transferred from the Indian Staff Corps to Her Majesty's Line regiments, and officers who resign the Service, will be entitled to receive back such portion of their contributions as may be in excess of the risk borne during their membership, but they cannot continue subscribers.

Half-Pay.

27. Married officers placed on half-pay may receive back such portion of their contributions as is in excess of the risk borne during the term of their membership, or they may continue to subscribe at Indian rates (which can be paid in India) for full benefits, or at lower rates for reduced benefits. In the event of their return to full pay, they shall, in addition to the subscription of their rank, be required to pay such donation as the circumstances of the case may demand. Unmarried officers may continue to subscribe at the European rate of subscription, and will not be entitled to any refund in the event of their withdrawal.

Retired Officers.

28. Married officers who retire from the service on a pension may continue to subscribe at the European rate of subscription, or they may withdraw, receiving back such portion of their contributions as is in excess of the risk borne during the term of their membership; but the usual subscription will be deducted from the pension of all such officers until they shall have intimated in writing their intention of withdrawing. Unmarried officers may continue their subscription on the same terms as married officers, but will not be entitled on retirement to any refund in the event of their withdrawal.

Dismissed Officers.

29. An officer who is dismissed the service by sentence of a court martial, or by other competent authority, will cease, ipso facto, to be a contributor under these regulations, and will have no claim to any refund. His family will not be entitled to any pensions under these regulations.

Certificates.

30. Declarations as to continued widowhood and the survival of children must be furnished to the Accountant-General, India Office, by whom pensions in Europe are paid under the authority of the Director of Funds.

Note A.—The contributions and donations to be paid by Native Members of the Covenant Medical Service are fixed for the present at an increase of 5 per cent. on the rates paid by Europeans.

APPENDIX.

Note.—In England all reports and applications for pensions must be made to the Director of Funds, India Office, London, S.W.

For the present, the rate of interest is fixed at 4½ per cent. per annum, and the rate of exchange with England at 1s. 8d. the rupee.

FORM (B.) of a Letter from an Officer reporting his having joined the service of the Government of India married. (See Regulation 14.)

To the Examiner of Fund Accounts for the Presidency of

Sir,—With reference to the Family Pension Regulations published with the Notification by the Government of India, in the Military Department, No. 1315, dated 28th December, 1872, I have the honour to report that I was admitted to the service of the Government of India, married, on the 18

The names and birthdays of my children are as follows:—

Sir,—With reference to the Family Pension Regulations published with the Notification by the Government of India, in the Military Department, No. 1315, dated 28th December 1872, I have the honour to report my marriage to Miss

A certified copy of the register of our marriage is herewith transmitted.

I was born on the
